names.name: Matico
names.ambiguity_note:
In Peru 'matico' frequently refers to Piper aduncum, not Buddleja
   globosa

names.name: Sangre de drago
names.ambiguity_note:
Argentine sources sometimes assign this name to Croton
   urucurana, a related but distinct species

names.name: Cola de caballo
names.ambiguity_note:
Name also applied to Equisetum arvense and E. giganteum in
   different regions

names.name: Jarilla
names.ambiguity_note: Name shared with Larrea cuneifolia and L. nitida in Argentina

names.name: Manzanilla
names.ambiguity_note:
Name also applied to Chamaemelum nobile (Roman chamomile)
   in some regions

names.name: Toronjil
names.ambiguity_note:
In some Andean countries 'toronjil' may refer to Agastache
   mexicana or local Lamiaceae species

names.name: Menta
names.ambiguity_note:
Generic 'menta' may refer to M. piperita or M. spicata depending
   on context and region

names.name: Hierbabuena
names.ambiguity_note:
Hierbabuena is predominantly M. spicata in Colombia and
   Venezuela but M. piperita in Southern Cone

names.name: Muña
names.ambiguity_note:
Muña is applied to several Minthostachys species (M. mollis, M.
   setosa, M. spicata) across Peru and Bolivia

names.name: Pasionaria
names.ambiguity_note:
Multiple Passiflora species share regional names including P.
   incarnata and P. edulis

names.name: Maracuyá
names.ambiguity_note:
Maracuyá most commonly refers to Passiflora edulis, not P.
   caerulea; high ambiguity risk

names.name: Rompe piedra
names.ambiguity_note: Name shared with Phyllanthus urinaria in some contexts

names.name: Llantén
names.ambiguity_note:
Llantén used for both P. major and P. lanceolata depending on
   local availability

names.name: Molle
names.ambiguity_note:
Molle also refers to Schinus polygamus and related species in
   Chile and Bolivia

names.name: Huingán
names.ambiguity_note: Huingán in Chile more precisely refers to Schinus polygamus

names.name: Uña de gato
names.ambiguity_note:
Uña de gato is applied to Uncaria tomentosa AND Uncaria
   guianensis AND unrelated species including Zanthoxylum spp. and Bougainvillea spp.
   in different countries. High ambiguity.

names.name: Valeriana
names.ambiguity_note:
Several native Valeriana species (V. carnosa, V. macrorhiza)
   share this common name in Andean regions

medicinal.use: Traditionally used for digestive discomfort and as an antispasmodic
Used in Argentina and Uruguay as an anti-inflammatory infusion

aromatic: Dried flowers used in aromatic preparations and sachets

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used for anxiety, insomnia, and digestive complaints across
   Southern Cone
Used in Andean regions for fever and common cold symptoms

culinary: Herbal tea infusion; flavoring for desserts and beverages
aromatic: Lemon-scented leaves used in perfumery and aromatherapy

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used in Andean communities for altitude sickness 
   (soroche)

Used in Peru and Bolivia as an appetite suppressant and energy
   supplement at high altitude

Used in ritual and ceremonial contexts by Andean indigenous
   communities

culinary: Leaf tea (mate de coca) consumed throughout the Andes

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used in Peru and Bolivia to support fertility and energy in
   high-altitude populations

Used for hormonal balance and menopausal symptoms in traditional
   Andean medicine

culinary: Root consumed as food; prepared as flour, porridge, or dried

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used throughout South America for digestive discomfort,
   bloating, and colic
Used as a calming and sleep-promoting infusion across all regions
Topically used for skin inflammation in traditional practice

culinary: Herbal tea; widely consumed across all regions
aromatic: Essential oil used in cosmetic preparations

medicinal.use: Traditionally used for anxiety, nervous tension, and insomnia

Used in the Andean region for digestive spasms and stomach
   complaints

aromatic: Lemon-scented plant used in aromatic preparations

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used for digestive complaints, nausea, and headache
   across all regions
Used topically as a cooling agent for muscle pain in traditional practice

culinary: Tea; flavoring for confectionery and beverages
aromatic: Essential oil widely used in cosmetics and hygiene products

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used in Andean regions for digestive complaints and
   flatulence

Used in Peru and Bolivia for respiratory symptoms including cough and
   congestion

culinary: Used as a seasoning herb in Andean and Argentine cuisine
aromatic: Aromatic tea; used to flavor chicha and herbal drinks

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used for liver and gallbladder complaints across Southern
   Cone
Used as a digestive and choleretic in Chilean traditional medicine

culinary: Dried leaves used to flavor certain traditional preparations

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used across South America for menstrual irregularities and
   as an emmenagogue

Used in ritual and protective contexts across Andean and Criollo
   traditions

aromatic: Used in protective and ritual aromatic preparations

medicinal.use:
Traditionally used in Amazonian indigenous medicine for inflammatory
   conditions
Used in Peru for immune support in traditional practice

Traditionally used as a sedative and for sleep disturbances across South
   America

Used for anxiety and nervous tension in Andean and Southern Cone
   traditions

contraindications: Contraindicated during pregnancy — traditionally recognized as abortifacient
Avoid in breastfeeding
Avoid in patients with kidney or liver disease
Legal restrictions apply in most countries outside Peru, Bolivia, and Colombia
Avoid in cardiovascular disease
Avoid during pregnancy
drug_interactions: Alkaloids may interact with monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs)

contraindications: Contraindicated in bile duct obstruction
Avoid in severe liver disease

Avoid during pregnancy — uterine stimulant activity reported in traditional
   sources
drug_interactions: Use with caution alongside hepatotoxic drugs

contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy
Avoid in autoimmune diseases without medical supervision
Avoid prior to surgery due to potential anticoagulant activity
drug_interactions: Potential interaction with anticoagulant and antiplatelet drugs

contraindications: Avoid combining with sedative medications without medical supervision
Not recommended for children under 3 years
Avoid during pregnancy
drug_interactions: Potentiates CNS depressants including benzodiazepines and barbiturates

contraindications: Essential oil is toxic — internal use of concentrated oil is contraindicated
Avoid in pregnancy
Avoid in children at high doses
Fruits may cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
Related to poison ivy family — contact dermatitis risk for susceptible individuals
Avoid in individuals with known allergy to Asteraceae family (cross-reactivity risk)
Use with caution during pregnancy at high doses
drug_interactions: May potentiate anticoagulant therapy at high doses

contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy
Avoid combining with sedative medications
Avoid concentrated essential oil internally during pregnancy
